title: Active vs Passive Voice
learning_outcomes: Describe structural characteristics of Active vs Passive Voice.
Derive mathematical and programmatic bounds for Transitive verbs limitations, Passive conversion agent deletions, Style choices for business writing.

content_sections.title: Active vs Passive Voice
content_sections.content:
### Syntactic Alignment, Modifiers & Logical Transition Indicators
Verbal Aptitude studies grammatical structure, modifier bounds, and logical flow connections.

#### Subject-Verb Agreement with Intervening Phrases
Verbs must align with their subjects, not object nouns located in intervening prepositional phrases (e.g. "One of the servers *is* down," not "One of the servers *are* down").

#### Dangling Modifiers
A modifying phrase must refer to an active subject declared immediately after the phrase. If the subject is omitted, the modifier is left dangling:
*   *Dangling (Incorrect):* "While writing code, the system crashed."
*   *Resolved (Correct):* "While I was writing code, the system crashed."

#### Logical Transition categories
*   **Adversative:** Indicates contrast (e.g. *nevertheless*, *nonetheless*, *conversely*).
*   **Causal:** Indicates result (e.g. *therefore*, *hence*, *consequently*).

### Depth Analysis: Active vs Passive Voice
In production configurations, we analyze how Transitive verbs limitations, Passive conversion agent deletions, Style choices for business writing affects performance parameters. We evaluate memory layouts and consensus thresholds.

callouts.title: Common Trap in Active vs Passive Voice
callouts.content: A common mistake when analyzing Active vs Passive Voice is ignoring latency bounds and boundary edge cases during concurrent executions.

callouts.title: Production Scaling for Active vs Passive Voice
callouts.content: Production servers implement strict resource isolation and cache alignments to prevent performance regressions when running Active vs Passive Voice loops.
